title: Area of triangles
description: Practice finding the area of right, acute, and obtuse triangles.
topic_path: Module 5: Area, surface area, and volume problems
Topic A: Area of triangles, quadrilaterals, and polygons

assessment_items.question_content:
Look at the image below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the area of the triangle?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] units$^2$
hints:
If we add an exact copy of the triangle, we can make a parallelogram.

Then we can use the parallelogram's area to figure out the triangle's area.

[[☃ image 1]]

The original triangle has $\dfrac{1}{2}$ the area of the parallelogram we just made.

$\begin{align}
\text{Area of parallelogram} &= \text{base} \times \text{height}\\\\
\text{Area of triangle} &= \dfrac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}\\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 5\\\\
&= 3 \times 5\\\\
&= 15
\end{align}$
The triangle has an area of $15$ units$^2$.

options.alt: A scalene triangle. There is a dashed line that is perpendicular to the base from the base to the opposite vertex. An arrow shows the length of the base is labeled 6. The dashed line is labeled 5.

**What is the area of the triangle?**

[[☃ input-number 2]] units$^2$
hints:
If we add an exact copy of the triangle, we can make a parallelogram.

Then we can use the parallelogram's area to figure out the triangle's area.

[[☃ image 1]]

The original triangle has $\dfrac{1}{2}$ the area of the parallelogram we just made.

$\begin{align}
\text{Area of parallelogram} &= \text{base} \times \text{height}\\\\
\text{Area of triangle} &= \dfrac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}\\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 18 \times 15\\\\
&= 9 \times 15\\\\
&= 135
\end{align}$
The triangle has an area of $135$ units$^2$.

options.alt: A scalene triangle. There is a dashed line that is perpendicular to the base from the base to the opposite vertex. The dashed line creates two right triangles. An arrow shows the length of the base is labeled 18. The dashed line is labeled 15.

**What is the area of the triangle?**

[[☃ input-number 2]] units$^2$
hints:
If we add an exact copy of the right triangle, we can make a rectangle.

Then we can use the rectangle's area to figure out the triangle's area.

[[☃ image 1]]

The original triangle has $\dfrac{1}{2}$ the area of the rectangle we just made.

$\begin{align}
\text{Area of rectangle} &= \text{base} \times \text{height}\\\\
\text{Area of triangle} &= \dfrac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}\\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 4\\\\
&= 1.5 \times 4\\\\
&= 6
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The triangle has an area of $6$ units$^2$.

options.alt: A right triangle. The base is labeled 3. The height is labeled 4. The longest side is labeled 5.
